era_context: The Classical Greek world during the Persian Wars. The Achaemenid Persian Empire under Xerxes I is at its height, having invaded Greece with a massive military force. The Greek city-states, led by Athens and Sparta, are mobilizing for existential conflict. This year marks the pivotal moment of the invasion, with major naval and land battles determining the fate of Greek independence. Simultaneously, the Chinese Spring and Autumn period is in its final decades, with the state of Qi dominant in the north.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources for this period)
Northern Europe beyond Greek and Roman awareness
Central Asia and the Steppe (sparse contemporary documentation)

events.title: Battle of Thermopylae
events.description: A Greek hoplite force of approximately 7,000 men, including 300 Spartans under Leonidas I, held the narrow pass of Thermopylae against a vastly larger Persian army for three days (likely 18–20 September 480 BCE). The Greeks were eventually outflanked via a mountain path and forced to retreat, but the delay allowed Athens to evacuate and gave the Greek alliance time to organize. The battle became a symbol of Greek resistance and sacrifice, though its immediate strategic impact was limited—the Persians continued their advance into Attica.
events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in multiple Greek sources written within 50–100 years of the event. The date is fixed by Herodotus and corroborated by later historians. Archaeological evidence from the pass itself is limited but consistent with the narrative.

events.title: Battle of Salamis
events.description: The decisive naval engagement fought in September 480 BCE between the Greek fleet (approximately 370 triremes) and the Persian fleet (estimated 600–1,200 ships, though exact numbers are disputed). The Greeks, under the command of Themistocles and Eurybiades, used superior tactics and knowledge of local waters to defeat the Persian navy in the narrow strait. The victory prevented Persian amphibious operations and forced Xerxes to withdraw the bulk of his army to Asia Minor, effectively ending the invasion threat. This battle is considered the turning point of the Persian Wars and a foundational moment in Greek history.
key_figures: Xerxes I (Persian king, observing from shore)

sources.name: Aeschylus, Persae (The Persians) — a contemporary dramatic account performed in 472 BCE

events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in Herodotus and in Aeschylus's play, written only 8 years after the event by someone who may have witnessed it. The date and basic narrative are secure, though details of tactics and ship numbers remain debated.

events.title: Burning and Evacuation of Athens
events.description: Following the Greek defeat at Thermopylae, the Persian army advanced into Attica and burned the city of Athens, including temples on the Acropolis, in September 480 BCE. The Athenian population had been evacuated to the island of Salamis and to Troezen in the Peloponnese beforehand, largely on the advice of Themistocles. The destruction of Athens was a profound shock to the Greek world but also unified Greek resistance. The burned temples remained unbuilt for decades as a memorial to Persian sacrilege, becoming a powerful symbol of Greek determination to rebuild.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from the Acropolis (burned debris layers)

events.certainty_note: The burning is attested in multiple sources and confirmed by archaeological evidence of destruction layers on the Acropolis. The evacuation is well-documented in ancient sources.

events.title: Xerxes I Begins Retreat from Greece
events.description: Following the naval defeat at Salamis and the failure to destroy the Greek fleet, Xerxes I decided to withdraw the bulk of his army from Greece, leaving behind a force under Mardonius to continue operations. The Persian king departed for Asia Minor in late 480 BCE, reportedly concerned about the security of his empire and the possibility of Greek forces cutting off his retreat route across the Hellespont. This withdrawal marked the effective end of the invasion phase, though fighting continued into 479 BCE.
events.certainty_note: The retreat is well-attested in Greek sources. The exact timing (late 480 vs. early 479) is slightly uncertain, but the year 480 is the traditional date for Xerxes' departure.

events.title: Ostracism of Aristides in Athens
events.description: Aristides the Just, an Athenian statesman and general, was ostracized (exiled by popular vote) in 480 BCE, likely in the spring before the major battles. The ostracism was driven by political rivalry with Themistocles, who dominated Athenian politics at the time. Aristides was recalled during the Persian Wars to serve as a general, particularly at the Battle of Plataea in 479 BCE. His ostracism reflects internal Athenian political tensions even during the existential crisis of the Persian invasion.
events.certainty_note: The ostracism is attested in later sources. The exact year is sometimes given as 482 or 481 BCE in different sources, but 480 is a common dating. The political context is clear, though the precise date is uncertain by a few years.

events.title: Conflagration at Delphi (Sanctuary Threatened)
events.description: As the Persian army advanced through Greece, the sanctuary of Apollo at Delphi was threatened. According to Greek tradition, a miraculous storm or divine intervention (possibly a rockfall from Mount Parnassus) destroyed the Persian forces attempting to plunder the sanctuary. This event is recorded in Greek sources but is likely legendary or greatly exaggerated. The sanctuary remained in Greek hands and became a symbol of divine protection of Greek religion against Persian invasion.
events.certainty_note: The miraculous elements (divine storm, rockfall) are likely legendary embellishments. The Persians may have threatened Delphi, but the supernatural aspects are not historically reliable. This event reflects Greek religious interpretation of the Persian Wars rather than documented fact.

events.title: Simonides of Ceos Composes Victory Odes
events.description: The poet Simonides of Ceos, a contemporary of the Persian Wars, composed victory odes and elegies commemorating the Greek victories at Salamis and other battles in 480 BCE. His works, including epigrams for the fallen at Thermopylae, became foundational texts in Greek cultural memory of the war. Simonides' poetry helped shape how Greeks understood and remembered the Persian Wars as a defining moment of Greek identity.

sources.name: Fragments of Simonides' poetry preserved in later anthologies

events.certainty_note: Simonides was a contemporary poet, and fragments of his work survive. The attribution of specific poems to 480 BCE is based on their content and later sources, but the general fact that he composed victory poetry during this period is secure.

events.title: State of Qi Dominates Chinese Politics
events.description: In China, the Spring and Autumn period was in its final decades. The state of Qi, under Duke Jing, was one of the dominant powers in the Chinese world. The period was characterized by interstate warfare, diplomatic maneuvering, and the gradual consolidation of power among larger states. The year 480 BCE falls within the reign of Duke Jing of Qi (r. 547–490 BCE), a period of relative stability and power for the state.
key_figures: Duke Jing of Qi (ruler of Qi)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: The Chunqiu is a contemporary chronicle, though it is terse and often difficult to interpret. The Shiji, written centuries later, provides more narrative detail but is less reliable for specific years. The general political situation is clear, but specific events in 480 BCE are not well-documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The numbers of Persian ships at Salamis are heavily disputed. Herodotus claims 1,200 Persian ships, but modern scholars estimate 600 or fewer, suggesting Greek sources exaggerated Persian strength to magnify their victory. The 'miraculous' destruction at Delphi is almost certainly legendary—no contemporary Persian source mentions it, and it reflects Greek religious interpretation rather than historical fact. Some modern scholars argue that Thermopylae was less strategically significant than Greek tradition suggests, as the pass could have been bypassed; the real turning point may have been Salamis alone. The extent of the Athenian evacuation and the number of civilians involved remain uncertain.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 480 BCE is rich but heavily shaped by Greek sources, particularly Herodotus, who wrote 50+ years after the events. No contemporary Persian accounts survive; our knowledge of Persian strategy and losses is entirely filtered through Greek sources, which had incentive to exaggerate Persian strength and Greek heroism. The sources are also shaped by Athenian political interests—Themistocles' role is probably inflated, and Spartan contributions may be understated. Archaeological evidence from the Acropolis confirms the burning of Athens but provides little detail about the battles themselves. The narrative of the Persian Wars became a foundational myth of Greek identity, which may have distorted historical details over time.

graph_edges.note: The Greek retreat from Thermopylae allowed the Persian army to advance, but the delay enabled the Greeks to prepare for the naval battle at Salamis.
The Persian naval defeat at Salamis forced Xerxes to withdraw the bulk of his army from Greece.
The burning of Athens occurred during the same campaign as the Battle of Salamis.
Xerxes' withdrawal left Mardonius in command, leading to the continuation of the war and the Battle of Plataea in 479 BCE.
